blocks.body: Prepositions of Movement helps you show direction, path, and the result of movement with to, into, onto, through, across, and other forms.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Prepositions of movement show not just a place, but where something moves, which path it follows, and whether it reaches a result.","rules":[{"label":"To","color":"emerald","text":"To usually shows movement in the direction of a destination.","example":"go to school / drive to the airport"},{"label":"Into / onto","color":"blue","text":"Into and onto show a change of position: movement inside or onto a surface.","example":"walk into the room / climb onto the chair"},{"label":"Path words","color":"amber","text":"Words such as through, across, along, towards, past, out of help describe the path itself.","example":"run across the road / walk along the river / come out of the house"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Core idea and form
blocks.body:
It helps to see the pair position vs movement.

  • in describes a position, while into means movement inside: The cat is in the box. / The cat jumped into the box.
  • on describes a position, while onto means movement onto a surface: The cup is on the table. / Put the cup onto the table.
  • at describes a point, while to shows movement towards it: She is at the station. / She is going to the station.

blocks.heading: 2. Main uses and examples
blocks.body:
PrepositionIdeaExample
todestinationWe walked to the museum.
intomovement insideShe ran into the garden.
ontomovement onto a surfaceThe child climbed onto the bed.
throughmovement from one side to the other inside a spaceThe train went through the tunnel.
acrossmovement from one side to the other over a surfaceThey swam across the river.
alongmovement following a lineWe cycled along the beach.
towardsdirection, not always arrivalHe moved towards the door.
pastmovement beyond somethingShe walked past the bank.

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: go in the room for movement inside.
    Better: go into the room.
  • Mistake: go to home.
    Better: go home with no preposition.
  • Mistake: mixing up through and across.
    Better: through = inside a space, across = over a surface.
  • Mistake: thinking that towards always means arrival. In fact, it only shows direction.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• To = direction to a destination.
  • Into / onto = change of position.
  • Through / across / along / past = path of movement.
  • If the sentence shows movement, you often need a different preposition from the one used for position.
